content.text: Many applications require interpolation of functions of two or more variables: terrain elevation from scattered measurements, image processing, and data defined on grids. The key techniques extend one-dimensional methods through tensor products and triangulation.

content.title: Rectangular Grid
content.content:
A **rectangular grid** (or **tensor product grid**) in $\RR^2$ is the Cartesian product of two one-dimensional grids:
\[
\{(x_i, y_j) : i = 0, \ldots, m, \; j = 0, \ldots, n\}
\]
where $x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_m$ and $y_0 < y_1 < \cdots < y_n$.

We write $f_{ij} = f(x_i, y_j)$ for the data values.

content.text: The simplest method uses piecewise linear interpolation in each direction.

content.title: Bilinear Interpolation
content.content:
On the rectangle $[x_i, x_{i+1}] \times [y_j, y_{j+1}]$, the **bilinear interpolant** is:
$$

P(x, y) = (1-s)(1-t)f_{ij} + s(1-t)f_{i+1,j} + (1-s)t f_{i,j+1} + st f_{i+1,j+1}
$$
where $s = \frac{x - x_i}{x_{i+1} - x_i}$ and $t = \frac{y - y_j}{y_{j+1} - y_j}$ are the local coordinates normalized to $[0,1]$.

content.title: Properties of Bilinear Interpolation
content.content:
1. $P(x_i, y_j) = f_{ij}$ (interpolation at corners)

2. $P$ is linear along lines parallel to the $x$- or $y$-axis

3. $P$ is continuous across rectangle boundaries

4. $P(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 xy$ on each rectangle (degree $(1,1)$)
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(1) At $(s,t) = (0,0)$: $P = f_{ij}$. Similarly for other corners.

(2) Fix $t$. Then $P = [(1-t)f_{ij} + t f_{i,j+1}](1-s) + [(1-t)f_{i+1,j} + t f_{i+1,j+1}]s$ is linear in $s$.

(3) On the edge $x = x_{i+1}$ (where $s = 1$):
\[
P(x_{i+1}, y) = (1-t)f_{i+1,j} + t f_{i+1,j+1}
\]
This matches the value from the adjacent rectangle (where $s = 0$).

(4) Expanding eq:bilinear in terms of $x$ and $y$ gives a polynomial with terms $1, x, y, xy$.

content.text: Higher accuracy uses cubic polynomials, matching not just values but also derivatives.

content.title: Bicubic Interpolation
content.content:
**Bicubic interpolation** constructs a polynomial of degree $(3,3)$:
\[
P(x,y) = \sum_{k=0}^{3}\sum_{\ell=0}^{3} a_{k\ell} x^k y^\ell
\]
with 16 coefficients determined by matching 16 quantities at the four corners:

- Function values: $f$

- First partial derivatives: $f_x$, $f_y$

- Mixed partial derivative: $f_{xy}$

content.title: Bicubic Basis Functions
content.content:
On the unit square $[0,1]^2$, define the cubic Hermite basis:
$$
h_0(t) &= 2t^3 - 3t^2 + 1 \\
h_1(t) &= -2t^3 + 3t^2 \\
\hat{h}_0(t) &= t^3 - 2t^2 + t \\
\hat{h}_1(t) &= t^3 - t^2
$$

The bicubic interpolant is:
$$
P(s,t) &= \sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{1} f_{ij} h_i(s) h_j(t) \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{1} (f_x)_{ij} \hat{h}_i(s) h_j(t) \cdot \Delta x \notag \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{1} (f_y)_{ij} h_i(s) \hat{h}_j(t) \cdot \Delta y \notag \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{1} (f_{xy})_{ij} \hat{h}_i(s) \hat{h}_j(t) \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \notag
$$
where $s$, $t$ are normalized coordinates and $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$ are cell sizes.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Image Scaling with Bicubic Interpolation
content.problem: Digital images are grids of pixel values. To enlarge an image by factor $k$, evaluate the bicubic interpolant at $k$ times as many points. This produces smoother results than bilinear interpolation, which tends to blur edges.

content.title: Tensor Product Spline
content.content:
A **tensor product spline** combines one-dimensional splines in each direction:
\[
S(x,y) = \sum_{i=0}^{m}\sum_{j=0}^{n} c_{ij} B_i(x) B_j(y)
\]
where $B_i$ and $B_j$ are B-spline basis functions in the $x$ and $y$ directions.

content.text: For cubic tensor product splines on a rectangular grid:

content.title: Bicubic Spline Properties
content.content:
The bicubic tensor product spline $S(x,y)$ satisfies:

1. $S(x_i, y_j) = f_{ij}$ for all grid points

2. $S \in C^2$ globally (continuous through second mixed partials)

3. On each rectangle, $S$ is a polynomial of degree $(3,3)$
content.label: Theorem

content.text: When data is not on a regular grid, more general methods are needed.

content.title: Scattered Data Problem
content.content:
Given $N$ points $(x_k, y_k)$ with values $f_k$, find a smooth function $F(x,y)$ such that:
\[
F(x_k, y_k) = f_k, \quad k = 1, \ldots, N
\]

content.title: Delaunay Triangulation
content.content: The **Delaunay triangulation** of a point set is a triangulation such that no point lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle. It maximizes the minimum angle among all triangulations.

content.title: Radial Basis Function (RBF) Interpolation
content.content:
The interpolant has the form:
$$

F(x, y) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} w_k \phi(\|(x,y) - (x_k, y_k)\|)
$$
where $\phi(r)$ is a radial basis function and the weights $w_k$ are determined by the interpolation conditions.

content.title: Common Radial Basis Functions
content.content:
| Name | $\phi(r)$ |
| Gaussian | $e^{-(\varepsilon r)^2}$ |
| Multiquadric | $\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon r)^2}$ |
| Inverse multiquadric | $1/\sqrt{1 + (\varepsilon r)^2}$ |
| Polyharmonic spline ($k$ odd) | $r^k$ |
| Thin plate spline | $r^2 \log r$ |

The parameter $\varepsilon$ controls the shape.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The interpolation conditions $F(x_k, y_k) = f_k$ lead to a linear system:
\[

content.title: Positive Definiteness
content.content: The Gaussian and inverse multiquadric RBFs generate positive definite interpolation matrices for any distinct point set, guaranteeing unique solvability.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Multilinear Interpolation in $d$ Dimensions
content.content:
On a $d$-dimensional hypercube with $2^d$ corners, multilinear interpolation is:
$$

P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\mathbf{k} \in \{0,1\}^d} f_{\mathbf{k}} \prod_{i=1}^{d} \left[(1-t_i)^{1-k_i} t_i^{k_i}\right]
$$
where $t_i$ is the normalized coordinate in dimension $i$.

content.title: Bilinear Interpolation Error
content.content:
Let $f \in C^2$ on a rectangle with side lengths $h_x$ and $h_y$. The bilinear interpolant $P$ satisfies:
$$

\|f - P\|_\infty \le \frac{1}{8}\left(h_x^2 \|f_{xx}\|_\infty + h_y^2 \|f_{yy}\|_\infty\right)
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Bicubic Interpolation Error
content.content:
For $f \in C^4$, the bicubic interpolant satisfies:
$$

\|f - P\|_\infty = O(h^4)
$$
where $h = \max(h_x, h_y)$.
content.label: Theorem
